item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i needet to read it twice until i knew what was going on.

really good one

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't understand why it is an scp when I first read it, but now I do. really interesting idea.+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A story about the fight between an artist and capitalism - with the background of the anomalous world.

Interesting, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea of a trying to make something so mundane/unmarketable only for it to marketed as fantastical is the kind of artistic tragedy I live for. 10/10 concept, I love it.

I did a reading for it, thanks for the great story!
https://youtu.be/jWpYxRvkwCI

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The end of this sentence is funny if you think about it:

Several of Schröder's acquaintances re-established contact with him in 2015, when he returned to Frankfurt to work on what he described as a final, ultimate work of "anti-art", which would "defy advertisement and marketing," although he never divulged any specific details.

I wonder if prospective renters are similarly prevented from expressing any interest in living there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not, in fact it's extremely non-anomalous in every possible way (see below if you want an explanation).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm glad you enjoyed it and thanks to both of you for the thoughtful posts. I consider any of my SCPs making one person think to be successful.

reply
– hide block

Both of you are close to what I intended.

The "anomaly" is something that is not only mundane, but cannot be anything other than mundane, in both properties and description. This is also why nothing in the description explicitly links it to the Foundation or to the artist.

This is where the "anti-art" comes in, with the intention being a work of protest. I like barredowl's explanation but it is probably more sophisticated that what I was doing.

The question is whether it is possible to create something which is really unremarkable in any way, and in this case the answer was no, since it was so unremarkable it was special (hence why it is an SCP). This, and the apartment being advertised to the wealthy, in contravention of the artist's intent, led to the alcohol-related death.

The stuff in the missed details is spot on, except the luggage was only added in because the only picture I found had a luggage stand.

The number is meant to be just a normal apartment number (if you think it's too big I guess you could interpret it as Apartment 59/19 Whatever St, though I'm not sure how buildings would be numbered in Germany). It was only picked because I had 3919 and 4919 as building/architecture-related SCPs and get a kick out of continuing the chain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think you're close, but I think it's actually the opposite.

+ Another possibly erroneous theory
- Hide theory

The title is "Apartment 5919", without the word SCP before it, but the number is an SCP number. 5919 doesn't make sense as an apartment number, given the size of the building depicted. So what I think is going on is the opposite. The artist's creation does exactly the opposite of what you theorized, I think, preventing any importance being given to it, rather than giving itself importance.

I think it's an apartment whose only anomalous property is that you can't describe it as anything more elaborate than a simple apartment. Even describing this anomalous property isn't possible, or it would be in the article.

The MC&D cards, then, are the only thing left for me to figure out. I'm thinking that MC&D tried to do an exhibition of this renowned artist's latest and greatest work, but the properties of the apartment made it impossible for them to even explain what made the work notable enough to view.

The complete and utter failure of his ambition perhaps then drove Andrej to commit suicide.

Notably, though, both the fact that the Foundation documented it as an SCP and MC&D handed out invitations means that it isn't impossible for one to know what the properties of the apartment are. It only prevents you from "hyping" it in any way.

Very clever SCP. I really enjoyed working it out. I'm still wondering if there are any implications I missed.

Edit:
+ There were some details I missed
- Hide some details I missed

There's a three-year gap between when he created the apartment, and when MC&D distributed the invitations. It's clearly MC&D's invitation that caused him to commit suicide.

So, next theory is that MC&D managed to not only discover the apartment, but managed, by simply attaching their name to it, managed to successfully advertise the apartment to some of their high-profile clients. The apartment's complete inability to be referred to as anything exceptional could certainly be very useful. And this exploitation, this perversion of Andrej's intent to create something truly unexceptional is what drove him to suicide, I theorize.

I think that there might be more to it, though. The truly spartan interior of the apartment does make me wonder if maybe the anomaly actually prevents you from decorating it.

It states that the last resident moved out in 2018, and nobody else has moved in. The containment procedures state that nobody is prevented from moving in, so it is probably the anomalous property of the apartment that has prevented it from being rented out. Presumably the last resident was Andrej, as even saying that he lived in it would be a way of advertising it.

It is interesting that the properties of the apartment don't prevent the Foundation from using the standard format. It even has special containment procedures. Given the redundant descriptions of the caretaker, I think it is implied that the caretaker is an SCP asset, but they can't actually write that out.

Thinking about it, the fact that the Object Class is N/A is almost certainly an effect of the apartment, as even a Safe designation would imply the apartment has anomalous properties, and the apartment suppresses any suggestion that it is anything other than mundane. Probably the Foundation would prefer to give it a Safe or Euclid designation. As the effect would be very quickly realized by anyone attempting to "sell" a potential tenant on the property.

…actually the luggage stand kind of stands out. This apartment is being used as more of a hotel room, isn't it? That makes much more sense, and would be much easier to cover up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ooh, I think I get this one.

[+] reveal possibly erroneous theory
[-] hide theory

So this apartment seems to be non-anomalous, but the Foundation is giving it some significance by noting it in a formal SCP document. Schroder here seems to be related to the apartment, given his body's retrieval is featured in the document, although there is no explicit mention of him having any connections with the apartment. The article also mentions a project he completed in late 2017, although the document states that he was "unable or unwilling to describe what he had created".

Putting all of this together, I think it's pretty clear Schroder made the apartment, or at least granted it the certain quality which made it so notable as to be in an SCP document, and that this is the late 2017 project. Given that Schroder's oeuvre is described as "defying advertisement and marketing", I'd say his goal in making the piece was to depict a frank version of real life. However, instead of something mundane being granted importance by being in a museum or other context (think Duchamp's Fountain), the apartment grants itself importance. This is Schroder's response to "what he perceived as increasing commodification and consumerism within the art world": making something mundane with no embellishment.

Anyway, that got a bit rambly, but nevertheless, I very much enjoyed this SCP. Enthusiastic +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As much as I hate gimmicky titles, I have to admit: I adore this. It's very, very, very clever.
